EV Awareness & Safety
Getting Started Recognising an Electric Vehicle EV Identification by Vehicle Type Types of Electric Vehicle EV Components & Systems

EV Awareness & Safety

Build your knowledge of electric vehicles and learn the safety practices that protect you and your colleagues.

Getting Started

Welcome to EV Awareness & Safety

This training course is designed to improve your knowledge of Electric Vehicles (EVs) and make you aware of the associated risks, so that you may exercise appropriate care for yourself and others when working near an Electric Vehicle.

Important: This first level online course does not in any way qualify you to work directly upon the High Voltage systems on the vehicle. You should inform your colleagues of your current trained level and request further assistance from a suitably trained person when necessary.

Learning Objectives

Know About Types of Electric & Hybrid Vehicles expand_more

State how to identify electric/hybrid vehicles. Give examples of the electric/hybrid vehicles that are currently available. Identify alternative fuel source vehicles.

Understand the Hazards Around HV Electrical Systems expand_more

State the hazards associated with high energy electricity. State the hazards that may be present when connecting and charging electric/hybrid vehicles.

Know How to Work Safely Around Electric/Hybrid Vehicles expand_more

State safety precautions to be taken before approaching and working around electric/hybrid vehicles. State how to identify motor vehicle high energy cabling and associated components. State how the vehicle may be safely charged using an external source.

Your Details

Select Your Role

Choose the role that best describes your day-to-day work. This will tailor the course content to your needs.

Click on the most relevant role to you.

Glossary Available Throughout

Throughout this course, you will encounter technical terms. A full glossary of terms is available at the end of the course for quick reference whenever you need it.

Recognising an Electric Vehicle

Why Recognition Matters

It is important that you can recognise an electric vehicle when it comes into your workplace. You will then be ready to exercise the correct procedures that will be necessary for a safe visit.

Identifying features of electric vehicles will differ between manufacturers. This means that you may need to exercise your skill and experience to make sure that you know what type of vehicle you are looking at — getting it wrong could be risky.

Exterior of an EV

Interior EV Identification Features

Orange High Voltage Cables expand_more

High voltage cables in electric vehicles are brightly coloured (usually orange) to make them easily identifiable and to warn of danger.

Power Controller / Inverter expand_more

The power electronics controller contains the inverter to change AC to DC current and vice-versa, and the electronic controller that manages the power flow of the vehicle.

Warning Labels expand_more

Warning labels indicate the presence of high voltage components and hazards. These are placed on or near high voltage systems.

Government Vehicle Enquiry Website

The UK Government provides an online vehicle enquiry service where you can check any vehicle's fuel type using its registration number.

Visit vehicleenquiry.service.gov.uk to verify whether a vehicle is electric, hybrid, or conventional. This is a reliable way to confirm the vehicle type before beginning any work.

EV Identification Criteria

0 / 7 complete

EV Identification by Vehicle Type

Which Features Apply to Which Vehicle Type?

Different types of electric vehicles can be identified by different features. This table shows which identification criteria apply to each vehicle type.

ICE VehicleBEVHEVPHEV
Badging✗✓✓✓
External mains charging port✗✓✗✓
Instrument cluster warnings✗✓✓✓
Power flow / energy use screen✗✓✓✓
Bright-coloured HV cables✗✓✓✓
Green band on number plate✗✓✗✗
Warning labels✗✓✓✓
format_quote
Not all types of EV will have the same identifiers. Always exercise caution and verify the vehicle type before assuming it is safe.
— EV Safety Principle

Types of Electric Vehicle

Exploring Vehicle Types

ICE

Internal Combustion Engine

  • Powered solely by a petrol or diesel engine — no electric motor or battery for propulsion
  • Energy is produced by burning fuel in the engine and transmitted mechanically to the wheels
  • Emits CO₂ and other pollutants through the exhaust system
  • Typically noisier and less efficient than electric vehicles
  • Still common globally, but being phased out in many regions due to environmental regulations

BEV

Battery Electric Vehicle

  • Driven entirely by an electric motor, with no petrol or diesel internal combustion engine
  • Has a large capacity high voltage battery to provide the required driving range
  • Instant driving torque from the motor gives strong performance with no engine noise
  • The high voltage system can be identified by the orange or brightly coloured cabling

HEV

Hybrid Electric Vehicle

  • Usually fitted with an Internal Combustion Engine (ICE), high voltage battery and electric motor assembly
  • Some will use their electric motor simultaneously with the ICE to drive the wheels
  • When the engine and electric motor work together this is called a Parallel Hybrid mode
  • Other hybrids can use their petrol engine to charge the battery and never use it to move the vehicle — this is called a Series Hybrid mode
  • Some vehicles are able to switch between these 2 modes — these are called Series/Parallel Hybrids
  • The high voltage system can be identified by the orange or brightly coloured cabling

PHEV

Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicle

  • "Plug-in" refers to the fact that the vehicle HV battery can be charged using an external mains charger
  • The vehicle will also have a combustion engine
  • Has a larger capacity high voltage battery compared to a non plug-in hybrid, increasing the distance that can be driven using battery only power
  • Some types use a combination of the electric motor and ICE engine to drive the vehicle wheels (Parallel Hybrid)
  • Other types may use the petrol engine only for charging the battery, which then powers the electric motor (Series Hybrid)
  • The high voltage system can be identified by the orange or brightly coloured cabling

MHEV

Mild Hybrid Electric Vehicle

  • Uses a Combustion Engine
  • Can have a belt integrated starter generator that can turn the combustion engine for starting and provide extra power when pulling away or accelerating
  • Cannot operate in fully electric mode — the engine will work together with the electric motor
  • Typically operates at 48V, which is below the recognised definition of "High Voltage"
  • The 48 volt system wiring is often coloured purple or blue

EV Family Tree

Understanding how vehicle types relate to each other helps you identify what you are working with.

Vehicle Types: Advantages & Disadvantages

info
<p><strong>Battery Electric (BEV)</strong></p><p>What are the pros and cons?</p>

Advantages: No tailpipe emissions, greatest electric only driving range of all EV types, easy to drive.

Disadvantages: Reliance on charging network, battery repair costs compared to ICE repair costs.

local_fire_department
<p><strong>Hybrid Electric (HEV)</strong></p><p>What are the pros and cons?</p>

Advantages: Good compromise between range and clean running. Optimised use of both powertrains can lead to very high MPG figures.

Disadvantages: Extra cost of EV systems means these vehicles are often more expensive. Battery only range will be less than a fully electric EV due to smaller battery sizes.

pan_tool
<p><strong>Plug-in Hybrid (PHEV)</strong></p><p>What are the pros and cons?</p>

Advantages: Offers both range and clean running. Larger battery than non plug-in hybrids giving greater EV only range. Can be charged from the mains electricity supply.

Disadvantages: More expensive than petrol or diesel equivalents. Battery driven, electric motor only range will still be less than a fully electric EV due to smaller battery sizes.

bolt
<p><strong>Series Hybrid</strong></p><p>What are the pros and cons?</p>

Advantages: Good compromise between range and clean running. Petrol or diesel engine removes the worry of running out of battery charge.

Disadvantages: The wheels cannot be driven by the petrol/diesel engine at all, so a very low battery will need to continuously run the engine to recharge. Smaller battery capacity means less electric motor only range.

shield
<p><strong>Parallel Hybrid</strong></p><p>What are the pros and cons?</p>

Advantages: Petrol or diesel engine removes the worry of running out of battery charge. Engine and electric motor combination can be used to drive in the most efficient way.

Disadvantages: Cost of EV systems means these vehicles are more expensive. Smaller battery capacity means less electric only range.

warning
<p><strong>Mild Hybrid (MHEV)</strong></p><p>What are the pros and cons?</p>

Advantages: Saves fuel and reduces emissions using a starter generator system. Comparatively easy for a manufacturer to engineer this technology into existing vehicle types.

Disadvantages: Does not support purely electric drive. Components are more expensive than conventional types.

speed
<p><strong>Micro Hybrid</strong></p><p>What are the pros and cons?</p>

Advantages: Another name for stop/start technology — very cheap and basic fuel saving solution.

Disadvantages: Stretches the definition of "Hybrid". No purely electric drive available.

The Definitive Guide to EV Components

Each type of electric vehicle contains different combinations of key components. Understanding what is inside each vehicle type helps you assess the risks before starting work.

EV Components & Systems

Inside Each Vehicle Type

01 / 05

Fully Electric Vehicle (BEV)

Contains: HV battery pack, power electronics controller, electric drive motors, mains charging port, on-board charger, DC/DC converter, battery cooling system. Driven 100% by electric motor with a large capacity rechargeable high voltage battery.

02 / 05

Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV)

Contains: Internal combustion engine, HV battery pack, power electronics controller, electric motor/generator, DC/DC converter, fuel tank, battery and engine cooling system. Combines a petrol engine and electric motor with a smaller HV battery capacity.

03 / 05

Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicle (PHEV)

Contains: Internal combustion engine, HV battery pack, power electronics controller, electric motor/generator, DC/DC converter, mains charging port, on-board charger, fuel tank, battery and engine cooling system. HV battery can be charged by an external mains charger.

04 / 05

Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle (FCEV)

Contains: Fuel cell system, hydrogen storage tanks, hydrogen fuel filler, HV battery pack, power electronics controller, electric drive motor, DC/DC converter, battery cooling system. Driven 100% by electric motor; fuel cell converts hydrogen and oxygen into electric power.

05 / 05

Extended-Range Electric (EREV)

Contains: 100% electric motor drive with an external fuel-based power unit that drives a generator to charge the battery and extend the vehicle range. Combines electric drive with a range-extending combustion engine.

This reference table shows which components are present in each vehicle type. Use this to verify vehicle systems during inspections.

Components by Vehicle Type

Hazards & Legislation

Hazards Around High Voltage Electrical Systems

At the end of this section you should be able to state the hazards associated with high energy electricity and the hazards that may be present when connecting and charging electric/hybrid vehicles.

HSE Legislation

Voltages present in electric and hybrid vehicles are significantly higher (currently up to 650 Volts direct current) than those used in other motor vehicle electrical systems.

The UK Health and Safety Executive provides guidance on safe working practices for electric and hybrid vehicles. These guidelines form the regulatory basis for workplace safety around EVs.

Source: www.hse.gov.uk/mvr/topics/electric-hybrid.htm

ECE R100 Certification

High Voltage Definition expand_more

ECE R100 currently describes high voltage as: "The classification of an electric component or circuit, if its working voltage is between >60V and ≤1500V DC or >30V and ≤1000V AC root mean square (rms)."

Important Context expand_more

These values are used when referring to Electric Vehicles. Please note that high voltage is defined differently in other industries and standards. Always refer to the specific standard relevant to your work.

Risks of Working with Electric & Hybrid Vehicles

bolt

Fatal Electric Shock

The presence of high voltage components and cabling capable of delivering a fatal electric shock. The storage of electrical energy in vehicle batteries.

speed

Retained Voltage

Components that may retain a dangerous voltage even when a vehicle is switched off. Electric motors or the vehicle itself may move unexpectedly.

shield

Explosive Gases & Chemicals

The potential for the release of explosive gases and harmful liquids and chemicals if batteries are damaged or incorrectly handled.

memory

Medical Device Interference

The potential for the electrical systems on the vehicle to affect medical devices such as pacemakers.

hub

Silent Operation

The possibility of people being unaware of vehicles moving as when electrically driven they are silent in operation.

star

Manual Handling & Fuel Risks

Manual handling risks associated with battery replacement. Hybrid vehicles also have a normal fuel tank present on the vehicle.

Four Categories of Work

01. Valeting & Car Parking

Lower risk activities including moving vehicles, cleaning, and general site logistics.

02. Service & Repair (Non-HV)

Work on the vehicle that does not involve the high voltage system directly, such as body repairs or conventional servicing.

03. Service & Repair (HV System)

Work that involves or is near the high voltage system, requiring specialist training and equipment.

04. Emergency Response

Responding to incidents involving electric vehicles, including electric shock situations and battery incidents.

Emergency Response

50V

Can cause damage to the human body

>60V

Classified as high voltage in EVs

800V

Maximum typical EV operating voltage

What Is an Electric Shock?

Stopping the heart beating properly expand_more

Electric current can disrupt the heart's rhythm, potentially causing cardiac arrest.

Preventing the person from breathing expand_more

Electrical interference with the muscles controlling breathing can cause respiratory failure.

Causing muscle spasms expand_more

Involuntary muscle contractions can prevent the victim from letting go of the electrical source.

Secondary injuries expand_more

Burns, fall injuries, and other trauma are also likely consequences of electric shock.

Severity Factors of Electric Shock

The severity of a shock is determined by a combination of factors.

Low SeverityMedium SeverityHigh Severity
VoltageLowMediumHigh
Contact MaterialNon-conductiveLow-conductivityHigh-conductivity
Current PathFinger to finger, same handHand to footAcross the body
DurationMillisecondsFractions of a secondOne second or more

What To Do If Someone Receives an Electric Shock

Assess the Situation
Do not touch the casualty if they are still in contact with the electrical source — you are at risk of electric shock too.
arrow_downward
Disconnect the Power Source
For mains electrical systems (such as vehicle battery chargers for BEV & PHEV), turn off the power supply at the mains. For high voltage vehicle systems, the HV supply will normally be disconnected when the vehicle is switched off.
arrow_downward
Remove the Casualty Safely
If the casualty is still in contact with the electrical source and it is safe for you to do so, use a purpose-made rescue hook or dry non-conductive material to separate them from the source.
arrow_downward
Perform First Aid
Once you are sure contact has been broken between the casualty and the HV source, perform first aid as necessary and appropriate for the injuries sustained.
arrow_downward
Call Emergency Services
Call 999 for emergency help immediately.
arrow_downward
Prevent Further Casualties
Ensure no one else attempts to work on the vehicle until it has been made safe. It should be cordoned off and signage set out to alert others to the danger.

Considerations for Low Risk Activities

Safety Considerations for Everyday Work

bolt

Silent Running

People who move these vehicles around the workplace should be aware that others may not hear you approaching them. Be vigilant when driving electric vehicles near other people.

speed

Keyless Start Precautions

Remote operation keys that only need to be close to the vehicle for it to be powered up should be kept away from the vehicle. Use a signal blocker pouch when not in use.

shield

Pressure Washing Risks

Pressure washing has the potential to damage high voltage electrical components and cables. High voltage cables are usually brightly coloured to aid identification — take care to avoid them.

memory

No Running Engine Warning

People who work around Electric & Hybrid vehicles should be aware that the vehicles may not have a normal engine sound when powered on. The vehicle may be ready to drive without a running engine.

Equipment Quiz: Vehicle Identification Sign

Select the primary purpose for the vehicle identification sign shown below.

Tells staff that the vehicle might need charging radio_button_unchecked
Should be present on every electric vehicle entering a workshop for repair so that all staff are aware that it is an EV radio_button_unchecked
Allows the service advisor to find their cars easily radio_button_unchecked
Equipment Quiz: Barrier Chain / Cordon

Select the primary purpose for the barrier chain / cordon.

This is used to hang the signs on the vehicle radio_button_unchecked
Stops anyone moving the vehicle from the workbay radio_button_unchecked
Alerts others and prevents unauthorised access radio_button_unchecked
Equipment Quiz: Key Signal Blocker

Select the primary purpose for the key signal blocker pouch / case.

Prevents the key from being stolen radio_button_unchecked
Prevents accidental power up on keyless start vehicles by blocking the key receiving and sending signals to the vehicle radio_button_unchecked
Stops the key being lost radio_button_unchecked
Equipment Quiz: Rescue Hook

Select the primary purpose for the rescue hook.

To be used by suitably trained staff in an emergency to remove a casualty from the source of an electric shock radio_button_unchecked
To be used by the workshop manager in the event of an emergency to drag a technician away from an electric shock source radio_button_unchecked
To move the high voltage cables away from the car if they become short circuited radio_button_unchecked

Safety Equipment (Technical)

Equipment Quiz: Insulated Gloves

Select the primary purpose for the insulated gloves — Class 0 rated 1500V DC.

Keeps hands clean and dry radio_button_unchecked
Prevents the spread of infection radio_button_unchecked
Should be worn by suitably trained persons when working on the High Voltage system of the vehicle radio_button_unchecked
Equipment Quiz: Warning Signs

Select the primary purpose for the warning signs.

Alerts others to the risks that are present radio_button_unchecked
Stops anyone moving the vehicle from the workbay radio_button_unchecked
Prevents access to the workbay radio_button_unchecked
Equipment Quiz: Vehicle Status Signs

Select the primary purpose for the vehicle status signs.

Tells staff that the vehicle might need charging radio_button_unchecked
Placed on the vehicle to let everyone know what the condition and operational status is radio_button_unchecked
Allows the service advisor to find their cars easily radio_button_unchecked
Equipment Quiz: Do Not Start Sign

Select the primary purpose for the do not start / work in progress sign.

Should be used to prevent damage or danger being caused by unauthorised starting during the repair process radio_button_unchecked
Should be used to save fuel by asking people not to start the vehicle radio_button_unchecked
Should be used to keep the steering wheel clean radio_button_unchecked

Risks & Mitigation

Each operating condition or system in an EV presents specific risks. Match each risk to its source.

Risk to Operating Condition Mapping

Risk Mitigation Strategies

For each identified risk, the following mitigation strategies should be applied.

RiskMitigation
Unexpected movementPay attention to warnings and messages on the instrument cluster showing vehicle drive status
Silent operationBe vigilant when driving EVs near other people, ensure acoustic warning system is functioning correctly
Unexpected engine startRemove keys to prevent vehicle starting. Isolate HV system. Disconnect low voltage system. Set out signage.
Electric shockIsolate HV system. Disconnect low voltage system. Cordon off vehicles being worked on. Ensure only qualified persons work on HV system.

Charging Terminology

Understanding Electrical Terms

Voltage expand_more

Voltage is the term used to describe electrical pressure. ICE passenger vehicles use 12V. Trucks and buses usually use 24V systems. Mild Hybrids operate at 48V. Hybrid and fully electric vehicles operate at >60–800V.

Amps (Amperes) expand_more

Amps is the measurement used to work out electrical flow. There is a relationship between voltage, amps and the resistance in a circuit — the latter of which is measured in Ohms.

Watts expand_more

Watts refers to the energy consumption of a component. We are familiar with this in terms of bulbs and other household items. Power (Watts) = Voltage x Amps.

Charging Calculations

The reason we need to understand these values is because they are mentioned when it comes to system operation and charging.

Example: "The car has a 52kWh battery." This means the battery stores 52 kilowatt-hours of energy.

A typical home supply can charge at 2.3kW (calculated by multiplying the power output of 230W by the amps the cables can carry at around 10A). To charge a 52kWh battery from empty at 2.3kW would take approximately 22.6 hours.

The vehicle owner's handbook radio_button_unchecked
ECE R100 radio_button_unchecked

Glossary of Terms

EV Glossary (A–G)

Arc/Flash

A sudden release of electrical energy through the air when a high-voltage gap is breached. Arc flash can cause serious burns, injuries, or equipment damage.

Battery Cooling System

A system designed to keep the battery within a safe operating temperature.

BEV (Battery Electric Vehicle)

A vehicle powered entirely by an electric motor using energy stored in a battery. No internal combustion engine.

DC-DC Converter

Converts high voltage DC power from the battery to low voltage DC power (12V) for vehicle accessories.

Electric Drive Motor

An electric motor that propels the vehicle's wheels.

External Mains Charge Connection Port

The socket on the vehicle where charging cables are connected.

Generator

In hybrid vehicles, a generator (often part of a motor-generator unit) converts mechanical energy from the engine into electrical energy to recharge the battery or assist propulsion.

EV Glossary (H–O)

HEV (Hybrid Electric Vehicle)

A vehicle combining a petrol engine and an electric motor. The electric motor assists the engine.

High Voltage (HV) System

Electrical components and wiring operating at high voltage levels (generally above 60V DC), which can be hazardous.

Insulated Gloves (Class 0 rated)

Protective gloves that provide insulation against electrical shock, rated for up to 1500 volts DC.

Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)

A petrol or diesel engine that creates mechanical power by burning fuel.

Key Signal Blocker Pouch/Case

A protective pouch that blocks wireless signals from car keys to prevent theft or interference.

MHEV (Mild Hybrid Electric Vehicle)

A vehicle with a small electric motor that assists the combustion engine but cannot power the vehicle alone.

On-board Charger (Built-in Mains Charger)

Device inside the vehicle that converts mains AC power to DC to charge the traction battery.

EV Glossary (P–U)

PHEV (Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicle)

A hybrid vehicle with a larger battery that can be recharged by plugging into an external electric power source.

Power Electronics Controller

Device that manages electrical power between the battery and electric motor, including DC to AC conversion.

Rescue Hook

An insulated tool used by emergency responders to safely pull a person away from high-voltage components in the event of electric shock.

Traction Battery

The main battery providing power to the electric motor in an EV.

UN Regulation ECE R100

International safety standard for electrical safety in electric vehicles.

verified_user

Course Complete

You have completed EV Awareness & Safety. Review any sections as needed.
